data.title: Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
STRATEGY OVERVIEW

The Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund seeks to provide capital appreciation. Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in common and preferred shares of publicly traded domestic and foreign companies of all capitalization ranges in the pet industry. The pet industry includes companies that offer services and products for pets and pet owners ("Pet Parents"). The Fund is non-diversified.

INVESTMENT SCORECARD

The Gabelli Pet Parents Fund (MUTF:PETZX) was down 7.28% in Q1 2025. The largest contributors in the quarter were CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Vetoquinol S.A. (OTCPK:VETOF), and Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), which returned 52.79%, 7.94%, and 22.21% respectively.

While pet related stocks pulled back in Q1, our outlook for the industry remains positive. We anticipate 5%-6% growth in the pet economy in 2025, bringing the market to $380 billion. This will largely be through growth in pet healthcare, premium nutrition, and innovative pet technologies. Technological integration remains a critical driver of industry expansion. The proliferation of pet tech—including wearable health trackers, smart feeding solutions, and telemedicine platforms—is creating entirely new market segments.

Our investment strategy focuses on companies at the forefront of these innovations, ensuring we capture value from technological disruption. Approximately 70% of U.S. households feature animal companions, and the non-discretionary nature of pet-related expenditure continues to provide a buffer against economic fluctuations, offering stable revenue streams for companies operating in this space. We believe the Fund is well positioned and that active management, coupled with overall industry growth, provides an excellent opportunity for short- and long-term value creation.

Investments in foreign instruments or currencies can involve greater risks and volatility than U.S. investments because of adverse market, economic, political, regulatory, geopolitical or other conditions.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

CVS Health Corp. (5.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (CVS - $67.75 - NYSE) is a leading healthcare company that provides a wide range of services, including retail pharmacy operations, pharmacy benefit management through CVS Caremark, and health insurance through Aetna. The company is a large retailer of pet food and vaccinations. The company published positive guidance for 2025, with $5.75 to $6.00 of EPS and $6.5 billion of operating cash flow. We have a PMV of $83 for CVS.

Vetoquinol S.A. (2.5%) (VETO - $83.80 / €77.80 - LSE) is a global animal health company specializing in the development, production, and marketing of veterinary pharmaceuticals and health supplements for pets and livestock. The company collaborates closely with veterinarians to provide innovative solutions for animal care, including products for dermatology, dental care, and nutritional support. Notable offerings include Phovia, a light therapy solution for faster skin recovery in pets, and Zylkene, a calming supplement for dogs and cats. The company generated significant cash flow in 2024 through margin expansion and positive product mix. We have a PMV of €85 for Vetoquinol.

Nestlé (1.8%) (NESN.SW - $100.98 /CHF 89.34 - SIX) is a Swiss multinational food and drink processing conglomerate that operates globally, offering a diverse range of products including coffee, bottled water, infant formula, and frozen foods. The company is renowned for its extensive portfolio of brands such as Nescafé, Kit Kat, and Maggi, and it also has a significant presence in the pet care market through its subsidiary Nestlé Purina PetCare. The company produced strong cash flows in 2024 and guidance remains unchanged for 2025 despite commodity price movements. We have a PMV of CHF 96 for Nestlé using a sum-of-the-parts methodology.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS

Total Net Assets: $3.4 Million
NAV: $13.37
Turnover: (A) 8%
Inception Date: 06/19/18
Gross/Net Expense Ratio: (B) 6.07%/0.92%
SHARE CLASS SYMBOL
Class I: PETZX
Class A: PETGX

(A) For the twelve months ended September 30, 2024.

(B) As of the current prospectus dated January 28, 2025. Net expense ratio after reimbursement from the Adviser. Effective through January 28, 2026, unless terminated early by the Fund's Board of Directors.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS

Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund QTR 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year Since Inception (06/19/18) (C)
Class I (PETZX) (7.28)% 6.64% (0.98)% 10.46% 6.02%
Class A (MUTF:PETGX) (7.30)% 6.34% (1.23)% 10.29% 5.90%
S&P 500 Index (D) (4.27) 8.25 9.06 18.59 12.88

Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (a)(b) Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) The Fund's fiscal year ends on September 30.

(B) Returns would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC, (the "Adviser") not reimbursed certain expenses of the Fund.

(C) Performance prior to the commencement of operations on April 1, 2019, is from the Predecessor Fund, Gabelli Pet Parents' NextShares.

(D) The S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization weighted index of 500 large capitalization stocks commonly used to represent the U.S. equity market. Dividends are considered reinvested. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of the Fund before investing. The prospectuses contain information about these and other matters and should be read carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, please visit our website at Home.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Funds concentrating in specific sectors may experience greater fluctuations in value than funds that are more diversified. Investing in foreign securities involves risks not ordinarily associated with investments in domestic issues including currency fluctuations, economic and political risks.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) 9.6%
Chewy Inc. (CHWY) 9.5
Freshpet Inc. (FRPT) 6.5
IDEXX Laboratories Inc. (IDXX) 6.1
CVS Group plc (OTCPK:CVSGF) 5.7
Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) 5.6
Trupanion Inc. (TRUP) 4.9
Elanco Animal Health Inc. (ELAN) 4.6
Petco Health & Wellness Co. Inc. (WOOF) 4.0
Qurate Retail Inc. (QVCGA) 3.4
Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

Daniel M. Miller

Original Post

data.title: The Gabelli Focused Growth And Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
STRATEGY OVERVIEW

The Gabelli Focused Growth and Income Fund is a concentrated, actively managed strategy launched in January 2021. The Fund invests in a global portfolio of common and preferred equities, REITs, bonds, and other securities that have the potential for capital appreciation while emphasizing a high level of current net investment income. The Fund currently distributes its net investment income on a monthly basis.

INVESTMENT SCORECARD

The Gabelli Focused Growth and Income Fund (MUTF:GWSIX) increased 2.40% in the first quarter of 2025, outperforming the S&P 500 by 6.25%. The largest contributors to performance were AT&T Inc. (T) (8.0% of net assets as of March 31, 2025), Option Care Health (OPCH) (2.0%), and Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT, 5.6%).

After a bull market that lasted nearly 2.5 years, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) ended the first quarter in correction territory. Markets rallied early in the quarter, with expectations of easing regulation and a resurgence in the IPO market. This was quickly cooled by trade and tariff uncertainty, with the new administration taking aim at trade partners through drastic tariff increases. Uncertainty was highlighted in the bond markets as well, with the U.S. Treasury yield peaking at 4.80% due to inflation concerns, before receding to 4.20% when fears of a tariff induced recession took hold.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS

Total Net Assets: $48.5 Million
NAV (Class I): $18.89
Turnover: (A) 31%
Inception Date: 12/31/02
Gross/Net Expense Ratio: (B) 1.39%/0.80%
SHARE CLASS (C) SYMBOL
Class AAA: GWSVX
Class A: GWSAX
Class I: GWSIX

(A) For the twelve months ended September 30, 2024.

(B) As of the current prospectus dated January 25, 2025. Net expense ratio after reimbursement from the Adviser. Effective through January 31, 2026, unless terminated early by the Fund's Board of Directors.

(C) Another class of shares is available.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS

Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A) (B) Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

QTR 1 Year 5 Year 10 Year 15 Year Since Inception (12/31/02)
Class I (GWSIX) (C) 2.40% 11.51% 18.57% 4.43% 7.54% 7.45%

(A) The Fund's fiscal year ends September 30.

(B) Other classes of shares are available, with different characteristics. For additional information please contact your financial advisor or call 800-GABELLI.

(C) Returns would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC, (the "Adviser") not reimbursed certain expenses of the Fund. The Class AAA Share NAVs are used to calculate performance for the periods prior to the issuance of Class I Shares on January 11, 2008. The actual performance of Class I Shares would have been higher due to lower expenses associated with this class of shares. The Fund imposes a 2% redemption fee on shares sold or exchanged within seven days of purchase.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (A) (B) Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

We continue to search for companies with strong cashflows, contributing to risk adjusted returns. In this market, the only certainty is uncertainty, and we believe the Fund is well positioned to navigate volatile markets.

The portfolio has a gross dividend yield of approximately 7.2%.

The Fund is classified as a "non-diversified" mutual fund, so that a greater proportion of its assets may be invested in the securities of a single issuer than a "diversified" mutual fund.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Vici Properties Inc. (VICI) 8.6%
Energy Transfer LP (ET) 8.4
AT&T Inc. (T) 8.0
Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD) 7.6
Franklin BSP Realty Trust Inc. (FBRT) 7.0
Blackstone Mortgage Trust Inc. (BXMT) 5.6
XPLR Infrastructure LP (XIFR) 4.7
Dana Inc. (DAN) 4.2
Maple Leaf Foods Inc. (OTCPK:MLFNF) 4.2
Qurate Retail Inc. (QVCGA) 4.1

*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

AT&T Inc. (8.0% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (T - $28.28 - NYSE) is a leading global telecommunications company with a robust portfolio spanning wireless communications, broadband, and digital entertainment services. AT&T currently serves over 100 million customers across various segments, leveraging extensive infrastructure and technological expertise. The company's recent focus on 5G deployment and fiber optic expansion has positioned it well to capitalize on the growing demand for high speed internet and advanced mobile services. We expect AT&T to generate $124 billion in revenues and $46 billion in EBITDA in 2025. We see the wireless business as resilient in a potential recessionary environment with a predictable subscription model that represents over 80% of consolidated EBITDA. We have a PMV for AT&T of $33 using a sum-of-the-parts valuation.

Option Care Health (2.0%) (OPCH - $34.95 - NASDAQ) Option Care Health is the largest independent provider of home and alternate site infusion therapy services in the United States, delivering advanced intravenous treatments for acute and chronic conditions. With over 40 years of experience, it offers customized care plans, cutting-edge medications, and nursing support to improve patient outcomes while reducing hospitalizations. Option Care outperformed in Q4, with top-line growth of ~20% and gross profit expansion of 9%, increasing 2025 guidance. Management has historically outperformed guidance, and we have confidence that they can execute on their 2025 targets. We have a PMV of OPCH of $38.

Blackstone Mortgage Trust Inc. (5.6%) (BXMT - $20.00 - NYSE) is a leading real estate finance company specializing in originating senior loans collateralized by commercial properties across North America, Europe, and Australia. As a subsidiary of Blackstone, the largest global owner of real estate, BXMT benefits from extensive market knowledge and deal flow. Management has noted that liquidity has returned to the real estate market, and believes we are in the midst of a recovery. BXMT continues to return capital to shareholders through strategic buybacks as well as their dividend of $1.88 or a current yield of 9.4%.

Daniel M. Miller

Original Post

data.title: Altria: Wall Street Missed Half Of The Equation
data.description:
MO Stock: Why Wall Street Dislikes It

I last analyzed Altria Group, Inc. (MO) stock on 4/30. In an article titled "Altria FQ1: Total Shareholder Yield Exceeds 10%," I reviewed its FQ1 2025 earnings report. I rated the stock as a hold after weighing the profit pressures observed in Q1 and the attractive total shared hold yield.

Since then, a few developments kept evolving around Altria stock and motivated this update. In the remainder of this article, I will detail my thoughts on two of the key changes in my opinion: the newly released Consumer Price Index for Tobacco products and also the changes in our tariff situation. My verdict is that the market has either overlooked or misjudged the impacts of these changes and thus understated the return potential of MO's stock. This reexamination has thus promoted me to upgrade my rating for MO to buy.

As you can see from the data provided in the chart below, Wall Street feels lukewarm about MO (while Seeking Alpha authors love it). To wit, the Street assigned a "Hold" rating to the stock based on the opinions of 15 analysts over the last 90 days. The majority of them (11 of them) gave the stock a rating of "Hold" or lower.

I believe a key reason behind the above sentiment is the secularly declining volume for MO's traditional smokable products. Next, I will examine this factor closely and argue why this reason is debatable.

MO Stock: What Wall Street Missed

Their fear of the declining demand for smokable products is totally justifiable and is based on solid facts. For example, the next bar chart shows the result from a 20-year study of the per capita sales of cigarettes. You cannot argue against the secular decline, which has been quite drastic. To wit, sales volume has almost halved in the 20 years covered by this study, declining from over 420 billion to about 220 billion only. Also note that the self-reported smoking rates are also declining consistently. So, the concern over the secular demand of MO's smokable products is totally valid.

However, we would be missing the other half of the equation if we only examine the volume. Revenues equal volume multiplied by unit pricing. As such, to fully assess MO's profit outlook, we have to examine unit price as well. To this end, Fred has recently updated the data of Consumer Price Index for tobacco and smoking products (average of all U.S. urban consumers) for April 2025. The index started at 100, representing the average prices of smoking products in 1982-1984. With a reading of 1,616 per the April update, it's amazing (or mindboggling) to see how much the prices have gone up since then.

Here, let me narrow the timescale a bit in two ways. First, let's examine the trend in the past 10 years. The reading was around 921 in April 2015. Thus, the current reading of 1,616 translates into a price increase of 75.4%, way more than the decline in volume decline during this period. Second, let's examine the YOY change. The reading was around 1,534 in April 2024. Thus, the current reading of translates into a YOY increase of 5.34%, again more than sufficient to offset the expected volume decline (of around 2% global based on British American Tobacco's estimates) and also higher than the average inflation during the past year.

With this half of the equation considered, it is surprising to see that MO's profit has been growing in the long term against the trend of declining cigarette volume. As a textbook dividend stock, I consider its dividends to be the most indicative metric for its true economic earnings in the long term. As seen in the next table, its dividend payouts have been growing consistently at healthy rates in the past 3 years (with a CAGR of 4.31%, 5 years (with a CAGR of 4%), and also 10 years (with a CAGR of about 7.07%).

Later, I will explain why the market might have also misjudged the potential of its new products.

MO Stock: Return Projections

The market's concern has resulted in a very compressed in my model for MO. The stock is currently trading at a P/E of around 10x~11x depending on the EPS metric you use. To contextualize things, the chart below puts its valuation and return potential against the broader market. The workings of this chart have been described in detail in our earlier article. Thus, only the most relevant parts are quoted below:

  • The long-term ROI for a business owner is simply determined by two things: A) the price paid to buy the business and B) the quality of the business. More specifically, part A is determined by the owner's earning yield ("OEY") when we purchased the business. And that is why P/E is the first dimension in the chart. Part B is determined by the quality of the business and that is why ROCE (return on capital employed) is the second dimension in the chart.
  • The long-term earning growth rate is estimated by the product of the ROCE and the Reinvestment Rate.
  • Finally, long-term ROI = valuation + quality = OEY + Growth Rate = OEY + ROCE*Reinvestment Rate

At its current P/E of 10x~11x, MO's OEY is around 10%. With a stellar ROCE of around 139% as seen in the chart below, MO's organic growth rate would be more than 5% assuming a 5% growth reinvestment rate. As a result, the total ROI I expect from MO exceeds 10%, far better than what I expect from the overall market (of around 5%). As another reference point, Apple, another stock that features a triple-digit ROCE, is priced at a P/E that is about 3x higher than MO.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of other positives, I also feel optimistic about the growth of its alternative product lineup, such as the Oral Tobacco offerings, the on! Nicotine pouches, and also the NJOY e-vapor products. Some of these offerings have been troubled with illicit products lately, and this is where I think the market could have misjudged/overlooked another factor: the potential impacts of the tariff situation that has been evolving since my last writing.

MO stock prices suffered their fair share of volatility together with the broader market amid the tariff drama. However, the tariff disputes could end up being a net positive for Altria. As a possible scenario, the disputes could lead to increased freight oversight between the U.S. and its trading patterns, thus leading to reduced incoming illicit trade in the future.

All told, this reexamination on MO is triggered by two factors: the newly released Price Index for Tobacco products and the tariff disputes. Successful investing depends on seeing the full picture in all possible ways. That is why our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) pays special attention to nonlinear and second-order thinking. In the case MO, I believe Wall Street's prevailing sentiment either neglected or misjudged these factors, thus creating a compressed valuation for a high-quality stock. As such, this reexamination has resulted in a rating upgrade for MO to buy.

data.title: ChargePoint Q1 Earnings: Another Weak Quarterly Report
data.description:
While electric vehicles have become more common in recent years, charging company ChargePoint (NYSE:CHPT) has not taken advantage of this potential opportunity. The name, which saw its shares peak in the mid $40s in late 2020, has traded below a dollar for the past five months. This week, the company reported its fiscal first quarter results, and the numbers show that the business is still in a very troubling state.

Previous Coverage of ChargePoint Holdings

I last looked at ChargePoint back in December, at which time, shares had bounced a little thanks to a quarterly revenue beat. Unfortunately, the company had seen its top line estimates drop quite substantially in recent months, and it was still losing a lot of money. With ongoing cash burn leading to significant dilution over time and a weakening balance sheet, I thought the rally could be short-lived.

Since that previous article, ChargePoint shares have lost nearly half of their value, while the S&P 500 is only down a couple of percent. With the stock trading below a dollar per share for some time, another one of my negative potential catalysts, a reverse split, has seemingly become more likely, to maintain exchange listing requirements. That event could happen in the next couple of months if the stock doesn't start to turn higher rather soon.

ChargePoint Fiscal Q1 Earnings Report

For the April 30th ending period, revenues came in at $97.6 million, down 9% from the year ago period, and missing street estimates by about $3 million. Networked charging systems revenues dropped 20% year-over-year to $52.1 million, but subscription revenue did jump 14% to $38 million. As the subscription business became a large portion of the company's total, GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins each rose 7 percentage points, to 29% and 31%, respectively.

The problem for ChargePoint is that it still loses way too much money. Operating losses did improve to less than $54 million from $67 million a year ago. However, that's an operating margin of still well over negative 50%, and when you throw in other expenses like interest, net losses were over $57 million. Weighted average shares outstanding were over 459 million, well up from 423 million a year earlier, due to a combination of stock-based compensation and equity sales needed to raise some cash.

Unfortunately for ChargePoint, the current U.S. administration could be looking to significantly hurt electric vehicle adoption. A rollback of electric vehicle incentives would negatively impact sales, and thus the amount of charging needing to be done. We are also seeing more vehicle manufacturers gain the ability to use Tesla's (TSLA) supercharging network over time, reducing the need for third party charging solutions. As the chart below shows, revenue estimates for the company have taken a massive hit in recent years. This is for the company's fiscal year that ends in January 2026.

Things don't appear to be getting much better in the short term, either. For its fiscal second quarter, management guided to revenues of $90 million to $100 million. Even the top end of that range was well below the street's average estimate of $108.35 million, which itself would have represented a slight year-over-year decline. At the midpoint of its guidance range, ChargePoint could be looking at a double-digit percentage decline in revenues over the prior year period, as well as a sequential decrease in the top line.

Balance Sheet Continues to Weaken

In Q1 2026, ChargePoint reported cash burn of about $34 million. That was a nearly 50% improvement over the prior year period. However, some working capital items did swing in the company's favor, and there was another more than $9 million cash flow add-back from payment in kind interest. As the chart below shows, the net cash position is at its weakest point in some time, further straining the company's financial situation. In the last five quarters, the value of stockholder's equity on the balance sheet has dropped from $328 million to less than $119 million.

The good news for now is that ChargePoint's $150 million revolving credit facility remains undrawn and there are no debt maturities until 2028. The company also has access to an equity sales program that had about $150 million left at the end of January. However, that plan would be quite dilutive given a market cap of just over $300 million in Thursday's trade. It would not surprise me to see some stock sales in the coming months to help offset some of this ongoing cash burn, especially since it doesn't seem like net losses are going to improve dramatically in the short term. Tapping the credit facility is also possible, but that would result in further interest expenses and cash burn.

CHPT Stock - A Look at Current Valuation

Given the fall in ChargePoint shares on Thursday morning, the stock currently trades for about 0.62 times its expected annual revenues for the year. That's down considerably from the more than 1.1 times seen in my previous article. It is also a huge discount to fellow charging name EVgo (EVGO), which goes for about 1.45 times its respective fiscal year's (December 2025 ending) expected sales. EVgo is expected to show a lot more revenue growth in percentage terms over the next few years after 38% expected growth this year, as opposed to a mid-single-digit increase for ChargePoint.

Despite these ongoing troubles, the street has remained very bullish on ChargePoint shares, with the average price target of $1.14 as of Thursday morning, representing about 66% upside for the stock. However, analysts have seemingly been playing catch up with the drop in shares over time. Just a year ago, the average target was $3.26, and five years ago it topped out at $50.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

Wednesday's fiscal Q1 report from ChargePoint was really weak, with the company missing street revenue estimates and providing even worse current quarter guidance. While margins and net losses are improving, the company's financial situation remains quite shaky, and ongoing cash burn has put even more strain on the balance sheet.

With the company potentially needing to dilute investors in the short term, and the possibility of a reverse split increasing, I am keeping a sell rating on the stock today. Revenue estimates continue to fall, and electric vehicles seem to be a major target for the current US administration. For me to even think about changing my rating, ChargePoint management really needs to improve its financial structure, but that could take quite a bit of time and be really painful for current shareholders.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Tesla Losing A Trump Put?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tesla stock has wobbled as Elon Musk lashes out against the Big Beautiful Bill. (0:15) Trade deficit plunges in April. (1:53) Battle for 23andMe heats up. (3:48)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, is Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) losing the effect of a Trump put?

The relationship between Elon Musk and President Donald Trump appears to have entered a new phase of pronounced tension and public disagreement. The rift centers on Trump's tax and spending legislation, dubbed the "One Big Beautiful Bill," and a series of policy and personnel decisions that have appeared to have frustrated Musk, who is very focused on the negative impact on the U.S. deficit of the bill.

This week, Musk unleashed a series of sharp posts on X, calling Trump's hallmark legislation a "disgusting abomination" and accusing it of being filled with "massive, outrageous, pork-filled" spending. Musk argued the bill would massively increase the already gigantic budget deficit and could bankrupt America due to the level of spending in comparison to tax receipts. He even urged his followers to pressure Congress to "KILL the BILL".

House Speaker Mike Johnson plans to speak with Elon Musk on Thursday as they clash over the bill. That follows reports that Musk did not pick up a call from Johnson on Wednesday.

Digging into the tiff, the bill's proposed cuts to the electric vehicle tax credit may have caught Musk by surprise, according to Wall Street analysts. The latest reports also indicate that Elon Musk has frozen Trump's last $100 million commitment to the Trump re-election campaign after the bromance hit its rough patch.

Looking ahead, the public disagreement could be resolved in a flash, or Trump could use his considerable influence to make things a little trickier for Tesla and SpaceX (SPACE) as they look for an easy path with government regulators.

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) were down 6% early Thursday, after shedding 3.6% on Wednesday. The stock is down 35% from its post-election high of $488.54.

On the economic front, the U.S. trade deficit narrowed sharply in April to -$61.6 billion, compared with the -$118.1 billion expected and -$138.3 billion in March as imports plunged.

Imports were $351.0 billion, down $68.4 billion from March. Exports were $289.4 billion, down $8.3 billion.

Wells Fargo economists say the “the temporary first-quarter splurge by businesses pulling-forward demand to get ahead of tariffs has run its course.”

“The April data tell us the show was over in Q1. Sentiment around imports has certainly shifted. The import components of the ISM surveys point to further weakness, as do other comments from purchasing managers who feel their inventories are high, backlog is low and new demand is drying up. This suggests depressed activity and import-demand ahead.”

Among active stocks, shares of MongoDB (MDB) are jumping after first quarter fiscal 2026 results beat estimates and analysts maintained their bullish views on the stock.

Morgan Stanley analyst Sanjit Singh said cloud database Atlas revenue accelerating to 26% year-over-year (compared to 24% year-over-year in the fourth quarter) paired with record customer additions proves strong execution to start the year, which contrasts mixed investor sentiment and an undemanding valuation.

PVH (PVH) is under pressure after the apparel company set its full-year profit guidance below expectations. The updated outlook reflects an estimated net negative impact related to the tariffs currently in place for goods coming into the U.S.

The new outlook is for EPS to be in a range of $10.75 to $11.00 on a non-GAAP basis, compared to a range of $12.40 to $12.75 previously.

And ZJK Industrial (ZJK) surged after the company announced it is ramping up production to meet rising demand from Nvidia’s B40 project.

Nvidia is advancing the development of a customized AI accelerator, the B40 chip, specifically for the Chinese market.

In other news of note, Bankrupt DNA testing company 23andMe Holding (MEHCQ) is set to hold a second auction to sell its assets, including customers’ genetic data, after a group led by former CEO Anne Wojcicki submitted a $305 million bid, beating an initial $256 million offer from Regeneron (REGN).

Following its Chapter 11 filing in a Missouri bankruptcy court in March, 23andMe picked Regeneron as the successful bidder in the first auction held last month. Wojcicki cited irregularities in the initial auction, arguing that it was unfairly closed to favor Regeneron.

But a subsequent bid from TTAM Research Institute, a California-based nonprofit founded by Wojcicki, has prompted the company to seek permission from U.S. Bankruptcy Judge Brian Walsh in St. Louis, Missouri, to seek fresh offers from potential buyers.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, exchange traded fund flows reflected renewed investor optimism in May. After a sluggish April, ETF inflows surged to $86 billion last month, bringing the year-to-date total to $444 billion. This keeps the industry well-positioned for another potential $1 trillion year.

For May, U.S. equity ETFs attracted $25 billion in new assets, although this represented a below-average share of geographic allocations, as investors increasingly turned their attention abroad. Non-U.S. equity ETFs brought in $18 billion, bolstered by $9 billion into developed markets and $4 billion into single-country funds, which was the seventh-largest monthly haul on record for that category.

Bond ETFs experienced a significant influx on the month, pulling in $37 billion, their third-largest monthly total ever. Credit-focused ETFs rebounded strongly with $12 billion in inflows, while inflation-protected securities drew $2 billion, extending their streak to five consecutive months of net gains.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ingles Markets Is A Robust Candidate For Significant Upside
data.description:
Back in March of this year, one company that I decided to reaffirm as a ‘strong buy’ candidate was grocery chain Ingles Markets, Incorporated (NASDAQ:IMKTA) even though the business had been experiencing some weakness leading up to that point, I found myself attracted by just how cheap shares were and the fact that leverage is substantially lower than the leverage of other similar companies. Since then, the stock has underperformed the broader market, rising by only 1.8% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 4.4%. This can be attributed to the fact that fundamental performance continues to worsen. But honestly, even with that factored in, the stock remains attractively priced. In fact, I would even go so far as to call it a deep-value play. Because of this, I have no choice but to keep the firm rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

Checking out Ingles Markets

Since my last article on Ingles Markets, investors have only had access to data covering a single additional operating quarter. This would be the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue totaled $1.33 billion. This represents a drop of 2.6% compared to the $1.37 billion the company reported just one year earlier. Looking at the data provided by management, this decline was driven by weakness across three of the four product categories that the company makes available for shareholders to see.

The one bright spot was grocery revenue, which consisted of sales involving grocery products, dairy products, and frozen foods. This inched up from $490.5 million to $492 million. Unfortunately, the perishables category dipped from $349.1 million to $348.1 million, while fuel revenue dropped from $169.7 million to $150.6 million. The drop in fuel is not terribly surprising given how volatile fuel prices can be. The biggest hit for the firm, by far, involved sales of its non-food products. These include alcoholic beverages, tobacco products, pharmacy products, and health, beauty, and cosmetic products. Revenue here plummeted from $305.9 million to $286.2 million.

Management does not provide much in the way of detail when it comes to the drivers behind these changes. But they do provide other interesting data instead. In the image above, you can see a breakdown of their retail grocery sales. This excludes its ‘other’ sales, which involve the company's fluid dairy and shopping center rentals revenue. You can see that a lot of this sales decline can be attributed to certain one-time events. A lot of it, a total of $30.6 million, involves three stores that the company has that were damaged as a result of Hurricane Helene last year. Most of that pain would be from stores that, as of the end of the second quarter of this year, remained closed. The company also saw a $9.7 million hit associated with the change in timing for the Easter holiday. In fact, when you strip out all of these things, you only see an $8.8 million decline in comparable store sales. And seeing as how fuel sales dropped $19.1 million year-over-year, with that decline almost certainly caused by declining fuel prices, none of this is surprising to me. In fact, management said that total grocery comparable store sales, excluding fuel, popped by 0.8% year-over-year.

Even though these were one-time events, Ingles Markets had to deal with pain on the bottom line as well. Net income dropped from $31.9 million last year to $15.1 million this year. Some of this was because, in the second quarter of 2024, the company booked a $7.7 million gain involving assets that it sold off. This ended up being property that was adjacent to some of its locations. By comparison, this year, it saw a loss of $0.2 million from those types of activities. The company also experienced an increase in operating and administrative costs from 20.8% of sales to 21.8%. This was primarily because of higher repairs and maintenance costs of $2.8 million, and professional fees that were also related to the inclement weather last year that hit the company to the tune of $1.1 million.

Other profitability metrics for the firm also worsened. Operating cash flow, for example, fell from $70.5 million to $63 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $53.9 million to $46.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $66.4 million down to $52.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. This shows a similar weakness. As the image below illustrates, this can be attributed to some of the same issues that impacted the company negatively during the second quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. It is worth noting, however, that the firm did see a pretty big drop in comparable store sales of $153.5 million. That is inclusive of fuel revenue, which did decline by $53.2 million. The fact that there was an improvement in comparable store sales in the second quarter of the year compared to the first quarter of the year does make me feel a bit more optimistic.

As was the case with the second quarter of the year, the first half of the year in its entirety resulted in a decline in net profits for the business. Net income was cut by more than half from $75.3 million to $31.7 million. Some of this was because of weather-related issues, with the total impact there amounting to $8.2 million. However, the company actually enjoyed a reduction of $10.3 million when it came to salaries and wages because of the temporary closure of four stores, three of which remain closed today. So if anything, when it comes to the operating and administrative side of things, the aforementioned hurricane had a positive impact on the bottom line. The bigger problem, in addition to the decline in sales that the company experienced, was the fact that the firm booked an $8.3 million gain from the sale of disposable assets during the first half of last year. That compares to a $3 million gain this year. But again, the decline in revenue was the primary culprit here.

Even if we assume that these troubles will persist, and if we annualize the results that the company has seen so far for the year, we would get net profits for 2025 of $44.4 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $192 million, and EBITDA of $206.9 million. Using these estimates, and also using the historical results for the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced.

These are quite low from a valuation standpoint, with the one exception being the price-to-earnings multiple projected for 2025. In the chart above, I then took the 2025 estimates and compared Ingles Markets to five similar firms. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, two of the five competitors were cheaper than it is. But when it came to the other two profitability metrics, our candidate was cheaper than all but one of the firms.

There are some other ways that we should value the company. In the chart above, you can see the price-to-sales multiple of each of these businesses. Once again, only one of the companies ends up being cheaper than Ingles Markets. And then in the chart below, you can see the same type of analysis using the price-to-book multiple. This one is particularly interesting to me.

Normally, when you see such a drastic difference in the price-to-book multiple, it is because of heightened balance sheet risk. This would be most evident by looking at the net debt of the company in question. So, in the chart below, I then looked at the net leverage ratio for not only Ingles Markets, but also for the five firms that I stacked it up against. And what I found was that, by a pretty massive margin, Ingles Markets has far lower leverage than any of these other enterprises. The fact that shares are cheap and the balance sheet of the company is attractive is incredibly bullish to me.

Takeaway

From the data at my disposal, it seems to me as though the market continues to underestimate Ingles Markets. The company is on solid footing, though it has had a few setbacks. The good news is that most of these setbacks have been caused by one-time events that should eventually subside. Given the totality of the circumstances, I have no choice but to maintain Ingles Markets as a ‘strong buy’.

data.title: Halliburton: High-Potential Stock For The Next Oil Down Cycle, But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
Halliburton (NYSE:HAL) is a crucial company for the oil and gas sector that’s doing key “behind the scenes” work. This makes them exposed to the economy, meaning that if Trump manages to push oil/gas prices down, tariffs continue, OPEC+ keeps ramping up the production, this could become a very interesting play as well (alongside oil stocks) and a nice way to diversify our oil exposure. One major opportunity I see here - on top of a rebound in oil prices - is Trump's drill-friendly policies, which would imply higher CAPEX from oil and gas producers, meaning higher profits for Halliburton. However, I don't believe this is a buy yet since it probably still has a lot more room to fall, as current prices may not be supported by their current financials.

We see the revenue spread pretty well between Completion/Production and Drilling/Evaluation. As expected, the revenue and profit/free cash flow fluctuate based on the environment. After all, despite not selling a commodity, this company is tied to the performance of commodity companies. This is why, during the Q1 earnings call, they even warned about the impact of tariffs.

About 40% of their revenue comes from North America, while international represents the other ~60%, with Middle East & Asia representing their 2nd largest region. This can change, although it also means there's high exposure to NA, which can be bad if tariffs continue and countries retaliate.

Another thing to consider is that oil companies seem to focus a lot more on consolidation (i.e. mergers and acquisitions such as Exxon's (XOM) Pioneer deal, Chevron (CVX) buying Hess, Occidental + CrownRock (OXY), ConocoPhillips + Marathon Oil (COP), etc.). Although everyone (especially Petrobras (PBR) if you look into it) is investing in exploration/new projects, they seem to prefer the same approach as gold miners - consolidation - which adds nothing to the global supply.

Talking about this, Petrobras and many other institutions also expect the global demand for oil to peak around 2030-35, after which it would slowly and steadily decrease.

At the same time, projects are running out globally, and new production will likely be more expensive. I said before that what we now call cheap oil is limited, and new projects already seem to have higher costs due to various reasons (such as accessibility). Petrobras sees some replacement challenges, meaning that their current projects (operational + currently in development) aren’t enough to keep the production at the current level. This is why they’re expecting to spend ~$2 bil per year on exploration until 2029. And Petrobras is an industry leader in proven reserves-to-production ratio, so the general market will probably have even bigger struggles and higher needs for investments.

Note that Petrobras has been working with Halliburton so far and will likely continue to do it. In January 2025, Halliburton secured its largest service contract with Petrobras to date - a three-year agreement to deliver integrated drilling services on several offshore fields in Brazil - after securing a few other contracts with them last year as well.

They can make a good amount of money for a $16.85 bil market cap. Currently, the TTM Price/FCF ratio = ~7.2, which is actually very interesting and below their 10-year median of ~9.

Currently, that’s around their third-lowest ratio ever (exceptions being 2016 when the FCF was negative and 2020), and today is not even a significant downside. In good (recent) times, we’ve seen it even at 3x the current level.

But, don’t expect them to make this much forever. The past 2-3 years have been exceptional for the company as all oil companies ramped up spending after oil prices rebounded significantly from pandemic lows, peaking above $120 per barrel in mid-2022. That's twice the current level! Plus, CAPEX were much lower in 2020 due to the pandemic/lockdowns/etc. Together, these incentivized companies to invest in new projects. But, at the same time, interest rates are still very high, and CAPEX levels are still below the pre-2015 boom era, as companies focus on capital discipline and cost savings/cutting. So, I’d be careful with this ratio.

Based on their latest earnings report, we see that, financially, they’re in a very good position, with the current assets almost covering the current liabilities + long-term debt. The equity is no longer that far from the market cap, so they seem to be in line with most US oil producers, which makes sense. At current prices, an oil company like Exxon could even buy them if they want to save some costs. This company has quite similar margins (~8% levered FCF) compared to Exxon/Chevron/Shell (7-10%), so it wouldn’t be impossible to see them want to save on the long-term costs while also diversifying their own operations and benefiting from the competition.

The dividend + buybacks represent at least 50% of their annual FCF, of which about 25% (~$600 mil) went into dividends and the rest into buybacks last year. However, they recently ramped up the buybacks, getting to $1 bil TTM, meaning about a 6% buyback yield, on top of the 3.5% dividend. But, remember that if the FCF drops, these will drop as well (bad/normalizing environment). With oil at $60, we are already approaching a period of CAPEX-cutting for many oil companies, so Halliburton might be affected due to the lower spending from their clients.

Overall, I expect similar volatility to oil stocks, meaning they can be a great pick during bad times, as a rebound in the oil industry will come with higher spending, which translates into more profit for Halliburton - as seen in recent years.

Short-term, I expect them to continue to fall if Trump/OPEC+ keep pushing oil prices lower, making it a potential buy well below its average levels. Also, Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” approach could be great for them in the medium/long-term - even with prices falling in the near future - since that could require more services that Halliburton provides.

Another potential opportunity I see in the falling this much is the potential to become an acquisition target. Someone like Exxon/Shell (SHEL)/etc. could easily afford to buy them, potentially cutting some of their operational costs and expanding vertically. This could be especially likely to see from a company like Exxon, who planned to reduce their production costs significantly by 2030.

Valuation:

Note 1: their Price-to-FCF ratio has varied significantly over the past years due to an unusual environment caused by COVID-19 and the high volatility we've seen in commodities. I generally take a more conservative approach when it comes to commodities or related companies, so I will go with ratios below their 10-year median for my estimations.

Note 2: for their FCF assumptions, I will use Halliburton + the industry's historical data and try to adjust it to inflation. Despite it and acquisitions over the past decade, Halliburton's revenue is almost identical to back when we've had similar market conditions, so there shouldn't be that much unpredictability.

Scenario 1

Assuming that oil prices go down to something like $50/bbl (similar to 2016), which would imply issues and lower CAPEX for many oil producers, let’s say they get to $1 bil per year. Say that the ratio is at 10 - which seems like an okay average P/FCF/ ratio during the downcycle for a US oil company (although it can drop a lot more in the short-term if there is panic) - and that’s a $10 bil market cap. So, a price of $11.5 and a ~40% loss. However, a 50% average payout if I buy at such a level could add up to ~8.5% average yields, so the upside from here could be quite interesting, as we’ll see in Scenario 3.

And this is actually around their equity, so falling below this could even lead to them being the target of an acquisition.

Note: $50 is definitely not the bottom of oil - especially in the short term - so things can get much worse, of course. Their financial health allows them to survive for a while, however, but it wouldn't be pleasant for the stock given the likely negative reaction from the market.

Scenario 2

With oil at $65/bbl, I think it’s reasonable to assume that they can make around $1.7 bil on average, which is in line with the level from 2018 + inflation-adjustments when oil was at around $65. Most oil companies on the planet that I’ve seen should be comfortable at this level to invest enough and not need to cut the CAPEX at all. Say the ratio is at 12, meaning a market cap of $20.7 bil and a price of $23.82. This is around 20% higher than the current price, so it seems justified since oil is also lower than in this scenario.

Again, the P/FCF ratio is low now, but the environment has been favorable for them over the past couple of years, so their FCF might drop soon, depending on politics as well.

Scenario 3

With oil maintaining at around $80/bbl, they could probably make ~$2.5 bil since everyone would ramp up investments/production/exploration (similar to 2023 + inflation). Put the ratio at 15, since the market would probably love oil stocks at that level (even if this is still below their median), meaning a $37.5 bil market cap. That’s a price of $43.15, which is very similar to the peak we’ve seen in the past half a decade and a profit of around 120% from the current level.

As I said in Scenario 1, a 50% payout here is a ~12.5% yield for that price, plus potential for special payouts.

My entry target + conclusions:

So, Halliburton is a healthy and profitable company that’s vital for the industry. They’re one of the backbones of oil and gas, which I believe still has at least a decade of normal cyclicality in it (especially thanks to Trump and his oil-and-gas-friendly policies now) before seeing signs of a decline. In the meantime, they’ll have to look into ways of adapting to newer energy sources, but that’s probably a worry for the next 1-2 CEOs, not now.

Since I think there are better companies to buy if oil only dips instead of crashing (e.g. Petrobras (PBR)), I think I’d be as greedy as possible with this one. As such, my entry target would be something around $13-15 - as close to Scenario 1 as possible - to maximize the potential returns and margin of safety.

My rating: On the watch list. Not a buy for me yet.

data.title: Tesla: Robotaxi Is Set To Unlock New Quantum Leap
data.description:
Introduction

My March recommendation to buy the Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) dip was quite good and timely because it was a solid rebound from the $225 level and the share price is currently 47% higher. The good part is that the rebound is unlikely to be over because TSLA's share price is still below its fair value. Fundamentals are all good, with the big robotaxi catalyst expected over the next couple of weeks.

The competition in EV intensifies, but Tesla is also aggressively working on diversifying its business mix, which makes the company more resilient. Since there is less upside left on the table compared to March, I slightly downgrade TSLA. Therefore, it is currently just a "Buy."

Fundamental Analysis

Despite intensifying competition in the EV space, Tesla's profitability remains strong. According to Quant Factor Grades, profitability remains this stock's core advantage. Moreover, I want to emphasize the stability of Tesla's A+ profitability, as the grade was the same three and six months ago.

The company continues working on driving down cost per vehicle, which will pay off over the long term once deliveries numbers return to a sustainable growth path. Decreased cost per vehicle will help Tesla to fortify its operating leverage, which will be crucial when the monetary environment becomes more favorable to drive revenue growth.

In addition, despite the intense competition that weighs on average selling prices, Tesla's free cash flow generating capacity remains robust. The TTM FCF margin is positive ($3.3 billion) which helps the company to support its fortress balance sheet. Having such a robust financial position is a great asset while Tesla aggressively ramps up its innovation spending.

In addition, the management works really hard to improve Tesla's business mix. This will protect the company from competition risks in EV. The expected mid-June launch of Tesla's robotaxi offering is likely to be an inflection point. I am confident in Tesla's promising prospects in this domain because the company did a tremendous job from a technological perspective as its full self-driving, or FSD, accumulated billions of miles of supervised driving.

Of course, Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Waymo is ahead of Tesla, as it is already operating in several large U.S. cities. However, Tesla also has robust strategic advantages in the pocket. First of all, the company will operate its own cars, which is likely to be more efficient operationally and financially. In addition, the technology is different, which is another chance for Tesla to differentiate. Waymo relies heavily on high-definition maps and lidar sensors. Tesla relies on a vision-based system powered by AI and vast amounts of real-world data collected from these billions of miles of supervised FSD driving. I am not an expert in autonomous driving technology, but a more data-driven and AI-powered approach looks like a solid differentiator for Tesla.

Last but not least for the robotaxi topic. Even if Tesla struggles to beat Waymo and remains number two in the industry, it will still create substantial value for shareholders as the industry is expected to demonstrate explosive growth. We see that the robotaxi market is expected to reach $188 billion by 2034. My calculations suggest that capturing even a 20% market share will mean that the fair value of Tesla's robotaxi business will be around $415 billion in 2034.

Besides vast potential to capitalize on the potentially explosive robotaxi market, there are other TSLA businesses that gain momentum. The company's Energy business is demonstrating impressive revenue and gross profit dynamic. This has strong potential to create more value for the company as its gross profit is growing exponentially, meaning strong operating leverage. Powerwall deployments are also jaw-dropping, which means that the demand is really hot.

To finalize fundamentals, Tesla's business mix is rapidly evolving, which is crucial to make the company more resilient given intensifying competition in EV. The balance sheet is clean and positions Tesla well to invest heavily in bold and ambitious new projects. Therefore, I remain bullish.

Valuation Analysis

The company's forward P/E ratio setup remains very bullish. Despite the FY2024 actual P/E of 137, it is sound because it is explained by the projected rapid EPS growth forecast from consensus. Thus, the P/E ratio is poised to decline by around three times over the next five years. Given the company's aggressively improving business mix, this forecast looks reasonable. On the other hand, we have to admit that the current forward P/E trajectory is not that attractive as it was in March when the current P/E was below 100.

The DCF model also demonstrated that there is less upside left on the table than it was in March. Due to the stability in interest rates, the WACC remains the same at 7.4%. Despite significantly improving the business mix, the previously used 5.5% constant growth rate was already quite high. Therefore, I also reiterate the same constant growth rate to calculate Tesla's terminal value ("TV"). I do not consider this constant growth rate too high because there are Apple and Microsoft, the companies that maintain double-digit revenue CAGR for decades.

Revenue assumptions for FY 2026-2027 were obtained from consensus. My confidence in the reliability of these assumptions is supported by the fact that FY2026-2027 consensus revenue forecasts were formed from opinions of 20+ reputable Wall Street analysts. This makes the sample representative and reliable. For the years after FY2027, I incorporated a 25-basis-point yearly growth rate decline.

Tesla's historical average FCF margin is 5.48%. This was used as the base-year assumption. Forecasting a robust yearly FCF margin growth looks reasonable considering the company's exposure to several potentially explosive industries and the management's commitment to deliver operating leverage. Thus, I applied a 250-basis-point yearly FCF expansion. Seeking Alpha suggests that there are 3.22 billion outstanding shares of Tesla.

My fair share price estimate for TSLA is $473. The upside potential is 42%, which does not look too high since the stock already demonstrated even higher levels in December 2024.

On the other hand, it is crucial to keep in mind that any DCF model is extremely sensitive to changes in growth assumptions. This especially relates to the constant growth rate, as a slightly lower one (5%) significantly undermines the fair value and only 14% upside potential remains left on the table.

Mitigating Factors

History teaches us that Elon Musk is not only the visionary leader of Tesla, but also frequently is a threat to the share price. I am not an expert in politics, but I think that his involvement in this field was awkward, softly speaking. There were some really controversial moves from him during his not very long political career, which significantly undermined Tesla's image, especially in Europe. The fact that he recently announced that he is leaving DOGE gave some optimism, which already vanished after he made another quite politically loud tweet recently.

The competition in the EV industry continues to intensify. The biggest rival of Tesla, Chinese company BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF), is firing all cylinders. While Tesla experiences struggles overseas due to some of Elon Musk's political statements about Europe, BYD is quite aggressive in capitalizing on this strategic slip from Tesla. This company recently outsold Tesla in Europe for the first time ever. China is a threat for Tesla not only because of BYD, as there are several other players. Players like Li Auto (LI), XPeng (XPEV), or ZEEKR (ZK) are smaller but also quite aggressive and thrive to gain domestic market share at Tesla's expense.

Tesla's earnings surprise record is really weak, which is a red flag, to be honest. There were only five positive EPS surprises over the last twelve quarters, which is a poor performance. Elon Musk has been extremely good at "selling" new groundbreaking projects during recent earnings call, which outshined poor earnings surprise records. Nevertheless, while the market has been ignoring this adverse factor over the last several quarters, it certainly won't last forever.

Conclusion

The stock saw a sharp rally over a relatively short period, and there is not that much upside left on the table. I remain bullish due to Tesla's stellar fundamentals, but I am not buying TSLA as aggressively as I did during the Spring share price weakness. Therefore, I downgraded TSLA from "Strong buy" to just "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
